Contribution guidelines requiring Developer Certificate of Origin (DCO) sign-off for all commits.

# Purpose
The document outlines the contribution guidelines for a software project, specifically focusing on the Developer Certificate of Origin (DCO) process. The DCO is a legal attestation that contributors must include with each contribution to confirm their right to submit the code under the project's open source license. Contributors must add a `Signed-off-by` statement in their commit messages to indicate compliance with the DCO. The document provides the format for the `Signed-off-by` line and instructions on how to include it using `git commit -s`. It also specifies that contributors must use their legal name and the email address associated with the commit. The guidelines ensure that all contributions are legally compliant and traceable, maintaining the integrity of the project's licensing.
# Content Summary
The document outlines the contribution guidelines for a software project, focusing on the Developer Certification of Origin (DCO) process. The DCO is a legal statement that contributors must include with each contribution to confirm their right to submit the code under the project's open-source license. This process ensures compliance with licensing criteria.

The DCO requires contributors to add a "Signed-off-by" line in the commit message of each contribution. This line must include the contributor's legal name and the email address used for the commit. The format for the sign-off is: `Signed-off-by: Your Name <your.email@example.com>`. Contributors can use the `git commit -s` command to automatically include this line in their commit messages.

The document also specifies that if a contributor modifies an existing commit from another developer, they must add their own "Signed-off-by" line without removing the original one. This ensures that all contributors to a piece of code are properly acknowledged and that the chain of legal responsibility is maintained. The guidelines emphasize the importance of using a legal name and matching email address to avoid issues with continuous integration (CI) processes.
